extra.reasoning:
The variable severe_headache appears only in r6 (priority 100): IF (rash AND fever AND slurred_speech AND syncope AND severe_headache) THEN acuity := self_care. Because r6 sets acuity to the LOWEST level (self_care) and has the highest priority (100), enabling it via severe_headache overrides all higher-acuity rules, which violates monotonicity.

Counterexample baseline: rash=T, fever=T, slurred_speech=T, syncope=T, severe_headache=F, back_pain=F, vision_loss=F, confusion=F.
- With severe_headache=FALSE: r1 (fever, prio 3)->emergent, r5 (syncope, prio 3)->emergent, r2/r4 (prio 2)->urgent. r6 not triggered. Highest-priority winners (prio 3) yield acuity = emergent.
- With severe_headache=TRUE (only this flipped): r6's full conjunction (rash, fever, slurred_speech, syncope, severe_headache all true) now fires at priority 100, beating r1/r5 (prio 3). Result acuity = self_care.

Enum order low->high is [self_care, routine, urgent, emergent]. Acuity drops from emergent to self_care when severe_headache is set true, so acuity is lowered. The monotonicity property is violated. The witness_assignment gives the baseline (severe_headache=false) input; flipping severe_headache to true triggers the violation.
